text: expand on my previous inquiry. “What the actual fuck ?” He scooted next to me and sat on the dry sand, looking at the waters of Forgotten Cove. We sat at the bottom of a shoehorn, the land protruding out on both sides to create a sheltered bay. “I know it didn’t much taste like it, but that stuff is ambrosia. Nectar of the Gods.” “It’s vomit.” He laughed as if my response was deadpan wit and not utter confusion. “Sure is.” I spit again, unable to get the taste from my mouth. My legs were bleeding and there were angry welts, but none of the other injuries I’d experienced in my trance were visible. Had they even happened? “Sorry about that. I had to cut I Mávri Pórta from you before you discorporated.” He rubbed a sandy hand over his dark shag of hair. “That’s what I call the siphonophores in the cove. Means the black door . It’s Greek.” My head spun, my mouth tasted like sea rot, and the sun was baking me alive. “Siphonophores?” He reached into a big pocket of his waders and handed me a bottle of water. I grabbed it and guzzled half before he had a chance to respond. “You know the Portuguese man o' war?” “The jellyfish?” “Actually a siphonophore.” He spread his hands. “A super organism. Made up of thousands of microscopic organisms. Zooids. Kind of like different cells in the body, they each have a job. Locomotion, reproduction, digestion. Get it?” “Fine, yeah. But—” “Yeah, yeah. I know. But what the actual fuck?” He rubbed his hair again, transplanting even more sand to his scalp. “You know the stories? About Eskuatep? Delhyme?” My heart sped. “The disappearances you mean. Yeah, I’ve heard.” He nodded, still looking across the water. “Well. Now you’ve seen too.” He turned to me, his eyes serious and unblinking. “You’ve seen where the missing go .” # I explained this all to Vanessa as she ate the blackened chicken over rice. “Bullshit.” “It’s the truth.” Vanessa got up and peered into the water. She didn’t even wrinkle her nose at the smell that time. When her eyes found mine, they held a glint of curiosity. “What do you want me to do?” She still wanted to be convinced. “Simple.” I retrieved a four-quart Cambro food container and placed it at the foot of the chair, next to I Mávri Pórta. “ I’m going to let the nematocysts sting me.” “Nemo—?” “Never mind. Once I put my hand into the water, give it exactly one minute.” I pointed at my coffee table. “There’s a stopwatch over there. Use that. You’ll also need a scalpel, knife, whatever you want to use. After exactly one minute, you need to cut this thing off me.” Her mouth twitched into a tentative smile. “Yeah?” “Yes. Cut me . I’m confident you know how to do that without killing me because, well...” She spun her bowie knife with a flourish. “That answers why me ?” “Found my last assistant on the same fetish site.” “Why not hire a doctor or nurse?” “Tried. Surprisingly, they had some ethical issues with the whole thing. Either that or they thought it was a joke.” “You know, I’m not so sure I believe you either.” “Maybe. But you’re curious. Curious enough to let this play out.” “What can I say? You intrigue me.” She started toward me with her knife. “One last thing. Make sure to put on the big rubber gloves. You don’t want this thing touching you.” “Right.” She retrieved all the equipment, pulled the gloves on, and took her position in front of me. “Ready?” “Set.” She smiled and hit the stopwatch. “Go!” # The ichor I heaved out of me. That chunky green and black ooze. It was the key to everything. Benny wasn’t supposed to be on campus over the summer. No one was. They had all gone abroad for sabbatical. But Benny saw his chance for something more. He wasn’t actually a student at Eskuatep University. He was a genius, that was true. But he wasn’t rich. That second all-important prerequisite to become part of the elite pantheon of Eskuatep alumni. “I wriggled my way in,” he explained on the hot sand of the beach. “A janitor first. They kept me on the outside for a long time. Still do.” “But you were in.” “I was in,” He agreed. “And I saw what they were doing with the Siphonophores. How they used them. How they exploited them.” I looked over my shoulder at the strange, abandoned campus. “Where are they?” “Gathering knowledge. Gaining power.” # I plunged my hand into the slimy water and I Mávri Pórta lit up like a dread Christmas tree. Thread-like tendrils wrapped around my wrist and I imagined the thousands of nematocysts expelling like microscopic harpoon guns, barbed threads exploding toxins into my body. I screamed. No matter how many times I did this, I always screamed. Before my eyes rolled back, I saw a peculiar expression cross Vanessa’s face. As she stepped closer, watching my terror, a sexual hunger consumed her features. The smell of Forgotten Cove enveloped me, and for a second, I saw Benny as he was that day on the shore, explaining the lost mysteries of Eskuatep to me. The way they’d constructed the pathways to confuse outsiders. The hidden doorways into buildings. I saw myself as I was too. Younger, hopeful, curious. Looking for answers to an unsolved mystery. Not aware of the agony of discovery. Then the world fell away as I, for the hundredth time, glimpsed oblivion. Snakes of linked tendons slithered around me like a tornado of flesh. I smelled toasted marshmallow. The horizon boasted three suns, heat so intense it was ice against my skin. Time meant nothing in that space between places. There were occasions I thought I’d been lost and trapped, hours passing, before one minute expired in my world. Other times my
